        or interpretation of results
      & Resolve with evidence; consider additional
        analyses; consult a neutral expert \\[3pt]
    \textbf{Framing}
      & Disagreement over how to position
        paper's contribution
      & Consult target journal scope; summarize framing of
        comparable studies \\[3pt]
    \textbf{Stylistic}
      & Disagreement over sentence-level writing choices
      & Defer to the lead author; style is not worth
        extended negotiation \\[3pt]
    \textbf{Interpersonal}
      & Conflict rooted in prior relationship dynamics,
        credit concerns, or workload resentment
      & Address directly and early; do not let it
        contaminate scientific decisions \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
  }

  \medskip
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Separate \emph{positions} (what someone wants)
      from \emph{interests} (why they want it) ---
      interests are usually reconcilable
    \item Put manuscript's quality and reader's
      needs at the center of dispute
    \item Seek advice from neutral party (e.g., committee member) if direct resolution
      is failing
  \end{itemize}

\end{frame}


% ============================================================

\begin{frame}{Hierarchy and Power Imbalance}

  \footnotesize
  \textbf{Common Sources of Imbalance}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Career stage:} harder for student to
      push back on a advisors framing choices, even when
      student is domain expert
    \item \textbf{Disciplinary norms:} fields may have incompatible assumptions about authorship
      order, target journal status, publication timeline
    \item \textbf{Publication pressure:} misaligned incentives
      around when and where to publish can fracture teams
  \end{itemize}

  \begin{block}{Power Differentials Are Structural, Not Personal}
  Collaborations can involve genuine differences in career vulnerability and publication pressure. 
  Ignoring these dynamics does not neutralize them.
  \end{block}

  \textbf{Protective Strategies}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Document your contributions in writing as work proceeds ---
      do not rely on memory or goodwill at submission time
    \item Raise authorship expectations explicitly and early,
      before significant work has been invested
    \item Consider drafting the \emph{Authorship Contributions} statement early
  \end{itemize}

\end{frame}


% ============================================================
\subsection{Task Management}
\begin{frame}{Dividing and Tracking Contributions}

  \footnotesize
  \begin{center}
    \emph{Ambiguity about responsibility is a project risk}
  \end{center}

  \textbf{Task Delegation Principles}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Assign ownership, not just involvement} ---
      one person is responsible for each deliverable, even
      if others contribute
    \item \textbf{Be explicit about scope:} \textit{``draft
      the methods section''} is a task;
      \textit{``help with methods''} is not
    \item \textbf{Revisit assignments} as the project evolves ---
      initial task maps rarely survive contact with data
    \item \textbf{Distinguish writing tasks from research tasks:}
      the person who ran the analysis may not be the best person
      to write it up, and vice versa
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Contribution Tracking Tools}
  \begin{description}
    \setlength{\itemsep}{3pt}
    \item[\textbf{Google Sheets}]
      Simple shared task list with owner, status, and
      deadline columns --- low barrier, universally accessible.
    \item[\textbf{GitHub Issues}]
      Create tasks with milestone tracking;
      integrates directly with version-controlled repositories.
    \item[\textbf{Asana/Trello/Notion}]
      Visual project boards suited to larger or
      longer-running collaborations.
  \end{description}

\end{frame}


% ============================================================
\subsection{Authorship Conversations}

\begin{frame}{The Authorship Conversation}

  \footnotesize
  \textbf{When to Have It}\\
  \smallskip
  \textbf{Early} --- ideally before data collection begins,
  certainly before writing starts. Revisit at submission.
  (Conversations become exponentially harder once
  significant effort has been invested.)

  \medskip
  \textbf{What to Establish}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Who will be an author vs.\ acknowledged contributor?
    \item What is the expected authorship order and on what basis?
    \item What happens if contributions change substantially
      during project?
    \item Who is the senior and the corresponding author?
    \item What is the plan if a co-author becomes unresponsive
      before submission?
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Avoid}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Gift authorship:} including advisor/collaborator who did not contribute substantially/intellectually
    \item \textbf{Ghost authorship:} omitting those who  contributied substantially
    \item \textbf{Retroactive negotiation:} changing authorship
      order after submission without full co-author agreement
  \end{itemize}

\end{frame}

\subsection{Final Thoughts}
\begin{frame}{Final Thoughts}

  \begin{center}
    \emph{Collaborative writing is a skill that is eased by intentionality.}
  \end{center}

  \vspace{2em}
  \textbf{Key Takeaways}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Choose tools and establish norms early
    \item Communicate openly about expectations and contributions
    \item Address conflicts directly and with the manuscript's quality in mind
    \item Document major decisions and contributions openly
  \end{itemize}
\end{frame}


% ============================================================
% PeerReview
% ============================================================

\section{Peerreview}

\begin{frame}
  \centering
  \vfill
  {\Large\bfseries Peerreview}
  \vfill
\end{frame}


% -------------------------------------------------

% ============================================================
\subsection{Pipleine}
\begin{frame}{The Peer Review Pipeline}

  \footnotesize
  \textbf{What happens after you hit submit?}
  \smallskip
  \begin{description}
    \item[\textbf{1. Submission}]
      Manuscript enters the journal's editorial system;
      author receives an acknowledgement and manuscript ID.
    \item[\textbf{2. Editorial Triage}]
      Editor-in-Chief or handling editor assesses scope fit and
      minimum quality threshold. \alert{Desk rejection} occurs 
      often within days without external review.
    \item[\textbf{3. Reviewer Invitation}]
      Editor invites 2--4 reviewers; many (most) decline. This stage
      can take days to several weeks.
    \item[\textbf{4. External Review}]
      Reviewers evaluate the manuscript and return written reports,
      within 2--8 weeks (often longer in practice).
    \item[\textbf{5. Editorial Decision}]
      Editor synthesises reviews into one of four (or more) outcomes:
      \textbf{accept}, \textbf{minor revision}, \textbf{major revision},
      or \textbf{reject} (with or without prejudice).
    \item[\textbf{6. Revision \& Resubmission}]
      Authors revise and submit response letter.
    \item[\textbf{7. Acceptance \& Production}]
      Manuscript enters copyediting, typesetting, and proofing
      before online publication.
  \end{description}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Typical timeline:} first decision in 3--5 months;
  full acceptance in 6--18 months across all revision rounds.

\end{frame}


% ------------------------------------------------------------

\subsection{Editor's Perspective}

\begin{frame}{The Editor's Perspective}

  \footnotesize
  \textbf{What editors are looking at}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Scope:} does the manuscript fit the journal's
      aims and readership?
    \item \textbf{Significance:} does it advance the field enough
      to justify publication?
    \item \textbf{Novelty:} is it sufficiently distinct from
      recent literature?
    \item \textbf{Presentation:} is the manuscript professionally
      prepared?
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Why desk rejection happens}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Obvious scope mismatch
    \item Insufficient (perceived) methodological rigour on first read
    \item Incomplete submissions (missing figures, references, etc.)
    \item Manuscript prepared for a different journal
      (wrong format, wrong citation style)
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{What this means for you as an author}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Tailor your cover letter to the journal
    \item Polished submissions reduces editorial hesitation
    \item Desk rejection is typically not a judgement of your science ---
      it is usually a targeting problem
  \end{itemize}

\end{frame}


% ------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Reviewer's Perspective}

\begin{frame}{The Reviewer's Perspective}

  \footnotesize
  \textbf{What reviewers evaluate}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Conceptual framing, novelty, significance of research question
    \item Appropriateness and rigour of methods
    \item Validity of results and statistical analyses
    \item Interpretation: do conclusions follow from the data?
    \item Presentation: clarity, figures, writing quality
    \item Completeness of citations and engagement with prior work
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{What reviewers shouldn't be required to do}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Editing your manuscript for you
    \item Reading a poorly organised paper charitably
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Reviewers are busy volunteers}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Clear, well-structured manuscripts reduce reviewer
      burden and tend to produce more constructive feedback
    \item Reviewer fatigue is real: dense, difficult manuscripts
      are evaluated less generously
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Tip:} as you revise your writing, ask yourself --- \emph{am I making
  it easy for a tired expert to understand and trust this work?}

\end{frame}


% ------------------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Author's Perspective}
\begin{frame}{The Author's Perspective}

  \footnotesize
  \begin{description}
    \item[\textbf{Accept}]
      Rare on first submission. Minor corrections likely requested at proofing stage.
    \item[\textbf{Minor Revision}]
      The manuscript is broadly acceptable; targeted changes
      required. Typical turnaround: 2--4 weeks.
    \item[\textbf{Major Revision}]
      Substantial work required (new analyses, restructuring,
      additional experiments). Usually one more full review round.
      Typical turnaround: 4--12 weeks.
      \emph{This is the most common outcome.}
    \item[\textbf{Reject}]
      The manuscript will not be reconsidered at this journal.%
      \footnote{\scriptsize Entirely new manuscript expected for "rejection without prejudice." }
      May include encouragement to resubmit elsewhere.
  \end{description}
\end{frame}


% ------------------------------------------------------------
\subsubsection{First steps}
\begin{frame}{First steps}
  \textbf{First steps after receiving reviews}
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item Read once for overall tone --- then \textbf{step away}
    \item Return the next day and read for content, not emotion
    \item Identify which comments require new analysis, which
      require writing, and which require clarification only
    \item Distribute your synthesis and discuss a plan with co-authors \emph{before} revising
  \end{enumerate}
\end{frame}

% ------------------------------------------------------------

\subsubsection{Rejection Management}

\begin{frame}{Rejection Management}

  \footnotesize
  \textbf{Rejection is normal --- not exceptional}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Rates can exceed 70--90\%; desk rejection of 50\%+ at high-impact journals
    \item Most published papers were rejected at least once
    \item Rejection does not mean the work is unpublishable ---
      it usually means the targeting was wrong
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{What to do immediately after rejection}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Read the decision letter and reviews carefully ---
      even a rejection often contains actionable feedback
    \item Discuss with co-authors: is the feedback
      \emph{scientific} (revise before resubmitting) or
      \emph{editorial} (wrong journal)?
    \item Identify the next target journal \emph{before}
      making any revisions
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Appealing:} 
      appropriate if review contains clear and foundational factual error
      or demonstrable misreading of the work.
      Rarely successful, can take weeks
      or months, and delays resubmission elsewhere

  \medskip
  \alert{\textbf{Rule of thumb:}} 
  Rejection sucks.  
  Get over it and aim to submit a rejected manuscript elsewhere within 4--6 weeks. 
  \textbf{Momentum matters}.

\end{frame}
% ------------------------------------------------------------



\subsection{Revising \& Resubmitting}

\subsubsection{Workflow}

\begin{frame}{Revision \& Resubmission Workflow}

  \footnotesize
  \textbf{Organise before you write}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Extract all editor and reviewer comments into a
      single numbered list
    \item Identify overlap and dependencies: 
      \begin{itemize}
        \scriptsize
        \item Overlap across reviewers' comments
        \item Revisions requiring new analyses prior to writing 
      \end{itemize}
    \item Set a revision timeline (considering journal's request); 
    request extension early if needed
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Revise manuscript and draft response letter in parallel}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Draft response as each comment is addressed, not retrospectively
    \item Track changes in manuscript for co-authors
    \item Keep change-tracked version for editor
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Verify}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Every response references a line number in the revision
    \item Every comment has a response {\scriptsize (even just "\textit{Implemented on line x.}")}
    \item Changes haven't broken figure or table references
  \end{itemize}

\end{frame}



\subsubsection{Response Letter}

\begin{frame}{Responding to Reviewer Feedback}

  \footnotesize
  \textbf{The response document is as important as the revision}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Editors often read your response \emph{before} re-reading
      the manuscript --- make it clear and complete
    \item Address \textbf{every} comment, even if you disagree
    \item Use a structured format: quote the comment, state your
      response, indicate the change made and its location%
      \footnote{\scriptsize See provided \LaTeX\ template.}
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Handle disagreement professionally}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item You may respectfully decline a reviewer request ---
      but you must explain why clearly and with evidence
    \item Phrase pushback as: \emph{``We respectfully disagree
      because\ldots and instead propose\ldots''}
    \item Never argue with tone; argue with data and logic
    \item If two reviewers contradict each other, flag it to
      the editor, use it to your advantage (if appropriate), and propose a resolution
  \end{itemize}

\end{frame}



% ------------------------------------------------------------

\subsection{Proofing Process}

\begin{frame}{The Proofing Process}

  \footnotesize
  \medskip
  Proofing is a \textbf{correction stage}, not a revision stage.\\
  Increasing done through journal's online proofing system.

  \medskip
  \textbf{Read very carefully, especially...}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item All author names, affiliations, and corresponding
      author details
    \item All equation, special character, and Greek symbol
      rendering
    \item Figures and tables in the correct order, at
      sufficient resolution, correctly captioned
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Practical notes}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Proofing deadlines are typically 48--72 hours
    \item Read proof against manuscript line by line, not from memory
    \item Uncaught errors become permanent in published record; corrections require formal erratum
  \end{itemize}

  \begin{center}
  {\large \alert{\textbf{Remember to celebrate!}} }
  \end{center}

\end{frame}




% ============================================================
% PeerIntroAnalysis
% ============================================================

\section{Peerintroanalysis}

\begin{frame}
  \centering
  \vfill
  {\Large\bfseries Peerintroanalysis}
  \vfill
\end{frame}


% -------------------------------------------------

% ============================================================
\begin{frame}{Analysis of Peer Introductions}

  \textbf{Group format}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Work in groups of three.
    \item Spend 5--10 minutes reading each peer's introduction draft.
    \item For each of your two peers, spend 15 minutes writing feedback.
  \end{itemize}

    After everyone is finished (35--40 minutes), take up to 10 minutes to explain your answers to each peer (about 30 minutes total).

    \begin{block}{Focus of this activity}
    We are not evaluating scientific correctness.
    We are evaluating clarity, structure, and audience engagement.
  \end{block}

\end{frame}

% ============================================================
\begin{frame}{Feedback Questions}


  \textbf{For each peer's introduction, answer:}
  \begin{enumerate}
    \item What did the author do well?
    \item Was the topic introduced compellingly and at the right level?
      Suggest one revision to improve engagement and/or audience reach.
    \item Was the manuscript's specific question clear?
      Paraphrase it in 1--3 sentences.
    \item Was the logical flow clear? Summarize logic in 4--8 words
      (e.g., x $\rightarrow$ y $\rightarrow$ z).
      Identify one place that could be clarified.
    \item If the Introduction ended with a take-home message,
      what was it? Paraphrase in 1--3 sentences and assess whether
      it matched expectations created by the rest of the Introduction.
  \end{enumerate}

\end{frame}

% ============================================================
\begin{frame}{Debrief and Reflection}

  \begin{itemize}
    \item What were common suggestions?
    \item What did you learn about your own writing?
    \item What characterized helpful feedback?
  \end{itemize}

\end{frame}


% ============================================================
% JournalPrep
% ============================================================

\section{Journalprep}

\begin{frame}
  \centering
  \vfill
  {\Large\bfseries Journalprep}
  \vfill
\end{frame}


% -------------------------------------------------

% -------------------------------------------------

\subsection{Friendly Reviewers}

\begin{frame}{Pre-Submission Review by Friendly Reviewers}
  \footnotesize
  \medskip
  \begin{columns}[T]
    \begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}
      \textbf{Selecting Reviewers}
      \begin{itemize}
        \item Avoid/Seek reviewers with strong prior positions on your findings
        \item Prioritize people who will give
          \emph{honest} feedback, not reassurance
        \item Consider who might make a better journal reviewer
      \end{itemize}
    \end{column}
    \begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}
      \textbf{Briefing Reviewers}
      \begin{itemize}
        \item State the target journal and audience
        \item Set a realistic deadline
          (2-3 weeks is standard)
        \item Clarify what kind of feedback is useful:
          structure, argument, clarity, or all three
        \item Identify specific concerns you want addressed
      \end{itemize}
    \end{column}
  \end{columns}

  \medskip
  Thank them by name in the Acknowledgments.

  Consider mentioning in Cover Letter.

  Friendly review is \emph{not} a substitute
  for rigorous self-revision. Send out only
  when you believe the manuscript is essentially complete.

\end{frame}

% -------------------------------------------------

\subsection{Final Formatting}
\footnotesize
\begin{frame}
  \frametitle{Final Formatting}

    \textbf{Things I ignore:}
    \begin{itemize}
      \item Font type and size
      \item Margin requirements
      \item Journal-requested citation format
    \end{itemize}

    \textbf{Things I don't ignore:}
    \begin{itemize}
      \item Word/page limits (\textit{incl. title, abstract, main text})
      \item Line spacing (\textit{double}) \& page numbering
      \item Figure placement (\textit{inline except if explicitly specified})
          and formats (\textit{pdf, tiff, eps})
    \item Cover page and/or end-of-manuscript requirements
    \begin{itemize}
      \scriptsize \item Author contribution and Acknowledgments
      \item Conflict of Interest disclosure requirements
      \item Data \& Code Availability statement requirements
    \end{itemize}
    \end{itemize}

    \textbf{Check:}
    \begin{itemize}
    \item Triple-read for spelling and punctuation
    \item Citation completeness and consistency
    \item Journal's double-blind requirements
    \end{itemize}
\end{frame}



\subsection{Author Contributions}

\begin{frame}{Author Contributions \& the CRediT Taxonomy}

  \textbf{Purpose}
  \smallskip
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Distinguish \textbf{authorship} from \textbf{acknowledgment}
    \item Increase transparency and accountability
    \item Expose \emph{ghost authorship} and \emph{gift authorship}
    \item Increasingly indexed by databases (e.g., OpenAlex)
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{The CRediT Taxonomy (14 Roles)} \href{https://credit.niso.org}{\texttt{credit.niso.org}}
  \begin{center}
  {\footnotesize
  \begin{tabular}{@{}ll@{}}
    \hline
    Conceptualization     & Methodology \\
    Data Curation         & Project Administration \\
    Formal Analysis       & Resources \\
    Funding Acquisition   & Software \\
    Investigation         & Supervision \\
    Validation            & Visualization \\
    Writing --- Original Draft & Writing --- Review \& Editing \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
  }
\end{center}

\end{frame}

\begin{frame}{Author Contributions Statement}

      \textbf{Common Formats}
      \medskip
      \begin{enumerate}
        \item \textbf{Prose (narrative)}\\
          \small ``J.S. conceived the study and led data collection.
          M.R. performed statistical analysis\ldots''

        \bigskip

        \item \textbf{Role-tagged list}\\
          \small \textit{Conceptualization}: J.S., M.R.\\
          \textit{Formal Analysis}: M.R.\\
          \textit{Writing--Original Draft}: J.S.\\
          \textit{Writing--Review \& Editing}: All authors
      \end{enumerate}

      \bigskip

      \alert{\textbf{Imperative:}} Agree on contributions \emph{before drafting}, not retroactively. 
      Disagreements are common, uncomfortable, and potentially costly.%
      \footnote{See \emph{Collaboration} class.}

\end{frame}
% ============================================================

\subsection{Open Science}

\begin{frame}{Open Science}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Promotes \textbf{transparency} and \textbf{reproducibility}
    \item Increases citation impact and research visibility
    \item Broadens engagement across the research community
    \item Enhances credibility
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Key Considerations}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Article Processing Charges (APCs) can be substantial
    \item Data sensitivity and participant privacy constraints
    \item Institutional and funder mandates increasingly require OA
  \end{itemize}

\end{frame}


\begin{frame}{Open Access Paper Publication Models}

  \smallskip
  {\footnotesize
  \begin{tabular}{@{}lp{2.2cm}p{5.8cm}@{}}
    \hline
    \textbf{Type} & \textbf{Who Pays} & \textbf{Description} \\
    \hline
    \textbf{Gold}    & Author / funder   & Published OA immediately by the journal; APC required \\
    \textbf{Green}   & Nobody            & Author self-archives a preprint or accepted manuscript in a repository (e.g., bior$\chi$iv) \\
    \textbf{Diamond} & Nobody            & Community- or institution-funded; no fees for authors or readers \\
    \textbf{Hybrid}  & Author            & Subscription journal offers OA for individual articles upon APC payment \\
    \textbf{Bronze}  & Nobody            & Freely readable on the publisher site \emph{without} open licence; access can be revoked \\
    \hline
  \end{tabular}
  }

  \medskip
  \small
  \textbf{Note:} Green OA costs nothing and is compatible with most journals.\\
  Check policies at \href{https://openpolicyfinder.jisc.ac.uk}{\texttt{https://openpolicyfinder.jisc.ac.uk}}.

\end{frame}



\begin{frame}
  \frametitle{Data \& Code Sharing}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Increasingly required by journals and funders
    \item Can be cited - Digitial Object Identifier  (DOI)
    \item Common repositories:
    \begin{itemize}
      \item Data: Zenodo, FigShare, Data One
      \item Code: GitHub, Zenodo
      \item Supplementary materials (\textit{no longer considered enough})
    \end{itemize}
    \item Best practices:
    \begin{itemize}
      \item Include comprehensive documentation and README files
      \item Provide appropriate licensing
    \end{itemize}
  \end{itemize}
\end{frame}


\begin{frame}{FAIR Data Principles}

  \textbf{FAIR} defines standards to maximise reuse and reproducibility.
  \medskip
  {\footnotesize
  \begin{description}
    \item[\textbf{F --- Findable}] Data are assigned persistent identifiers (e.g., DOIs)
      and described with rich, searchable metadata.
    \item[\textbf{A --- Accessible}] Data and metadata are retrievable via open, standardised
      protocols; access conditions are clearly stated.
    \item[\textbf{I --- Interoperable}] Data use common formats, vocabularies, and ontologies
      to enable integration with other datasets and tools.
    \item[\textbf{R --- Reusable}] Data carry explicit licences (e.g., CC BY) and sufficient
      provenance information for others to build on them.
  \end{description}
  }

  \medskip
  \small
  Full guidelines at \href{https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles}{\texttt{go-fair.org/fair-principles}}.\\
  \smallskip
  \textbf{Note:} FAIR does not require data to be \emph{open} --- sensitive data can be
  FAIR with controlled access.

\end{frame}

% -------------------------------------------

\subsection{Preprint Repositories}

\begin{frame}{Preprint Repositories: Examples}

  \footnotesize
  \begin{description}
    \item[\href{https://www.biorxiv.org}{\textbf{bioR$\chi$iv}}]
      Life sciences; operated by Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory;
      the dominant biology preprint server since 2013.

    \item[\href{https://ecoevorxiv.org}{\textbf{EcoEvoR$\chi$iv}}]
      Ecology and evolutionary biology; community-governed;
      supports registered reports and post-publication versions.

    \item[\href{https://arxiv.org}{\textbf{ar$\chi$iv}}]
      Physics, mathematics, computer science, quantitative biology;
      the oldest major preprint server (est.\ 1991); moderated by
      field endorsers.

    \item[\href{https://zenodo.org}{\textbf{Zenodo}}]
      General-purpose; accepts preprints, datasets,
      code, and presentations; assigns DOIs; widely used for
      open science compliance.

    \item[\href{https://www.authorea.com}{\textbf{Authorea}}]
      Collaborative writing and preprint platform; supports
      live figures, data embedding, and inline commenting;
      blurs the boundary between manuscript and publication.
  \end{description}

\end{frame}

\begin{frame}{Preprint Repositories: Primary Pros \& Cons}

  \footnotesize
  \begin{columns}[T]

    \begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}
      \textbf{Advantages}
      \smallskip
      \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Speed:} findings are public immediately,
          months before formal publication
        \item \textbf{Priority:} timestamped record of contribution
        \item \textbf{Visibility:} indexed by Google Scholar, etc.
        \item \textbf{Feedback:} community comments before peer review
        \item \textbf{Open access:} freely available
        \item \textbf{Funder compliance:} satisfies some open
          science mandates immediately
      \end{itemize}
    \end{column}

    \begin{column}{0.48\textwidth}
      \textbf{Disadvantages}
      \smallskip
      \begin{itemize}
        \item \textbf{Journal policies:} some journals
          discourage or prohibit
        \item \textbf{No peer review:} errors and weak claims
          are public
        \item \textbf{Perception:} some fields and hiring
          committees do not credit preprints
        \item \textbf{Retraction gap:} flawed preprints may
          persist even after journal rejection
        \item \textbf{Media risk:} premature press coverage
          before validation
      \end{itemize}
    \end{column}

  \end{columns}

\end{frame}


\begin{frame}{Peer Community In$\ldots$ (PCI)}

\begin{columns}[T, totalwidth=\textwidth]

\column{0.8\textwidth}

  \footnotesize
  \href{https://peercommunityin.org}{\texttt{PeerCommunityIn.org}}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Goals}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Transparent peer review \emph{before}
      (or \emph{instead of}) journal submission
    \item Eliminate fees for authors and readers
    \item Reduce dependence on commercial publishers
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{How It Works}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item Authors submit preprint (e.g., to bioR$\chi$iv), then
      request PCI review
    \item Volunteer recommender (acting editor) solicits and coordinates reviews
    \item Accepted preprints receive a \textbf{PCI Recommendation} ---
      citable endorsement
    \item PCI-friendly journals accept
      recommended preprints, or regular review
  \end{itemize}

\column{0.2\textwidth}
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figs/PCI_list.png}

\end{columns}

\end{frame}

% ----------------------------------

\subsection{Page Charges}

\begin{frame}{Page Charges \& Publication Fees}

  \footnotesize
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Article Processing Charges (APCs):} \$500--\$12,000+
      depending on journal/OA model
    \item \textbf{Page charges:}  \$0--\$200 per printed page for
      subscription journals
    \item \textbf{Color figure fees:} \$300--\$1,000+ per figure;
      often free online-only
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \textbf{Who Pays --- and How to Find Out}
  \begin{itemize}
    \item \textbf{Ask your advisor first:} most PIs budget for publication
      costs through grants; this is a normal conversation to have
    \item \textbf{Institutional waivers:} many universities (but not OSU!) have
      OA publishing funds; check with library
    \item \textbf{Funder mandates:} NIH, Wellcome Trust, and others
      \emph{require} OA and may cover fees directly
    \item \textbf{Request a waiver:} most journals offer hardship waivers
      for authors without funding --- ask before assuming you must pay
  \end{itemize}

  \medskip
  \alert{\textbf{Bottom line:}} You shouldn't be expected to pay out of pocket.
  Clarify funding \emph{before} submission, not after acceptance.

\end{frame}

% ----------------------------------

\subsection{Suggesting Reviewers}

\begin{frame}{Suggesting Potential Reviewers}

\textbf{How to Identify}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Researchers who publish directly on topic or methods
    \item Authors cited in manuscript (but not exclusively)
    \item Individuals with complementary expertise (theory, methods, system)
\end{itemize}

\medskip

\textbf{Who to Avoid}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Current or recent collaborators (typically last 3--5 years)
    \item Members of your institution or close professional network
    \item Advisors, committee members, or mentors
\end{itemize}

\medskip

\textbf{Strategic Considerations}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Aim for 4--8 suggested reviewers
    \item Ensure diversity (perspectives, institutions, career stage, gender)
    \item Avoid suggesting only highly favorable reviewers
\end{itemize}

\medskip

\textbf{Opposed Reviewers}
\begin{itemize}
    \item Allowed by some journals but use very sparingly and justify!%
    \footnote{\scriptsize{Consider use as \textit{"friendly" reviewer}}}
\end{itemize}

\end{frame}
